fact_a_true: Hydrogen is the lightest element and makes up most of the sun.
fact_b_false: Hydrogen makes balloons float and makes voices sound funny when you breathe it in.

fact_a_true: Helium makes balloons float and makes voices sound funny when you breathe it in.
fact_b_false: Helium makes fireworks burn bright red, which is why holiday rockets are so colorful.

fact_a_true: Lithium is the lightest metal and powers many phones, tablets, and electric cars.
fact_b_false: Lithium helps jet engines spin hot and fast without melting.

fact_a_true: Beryllium is a strong, light metal that helps build airplanes and spaceships.
fact_b_false: Beryllium is named after Nicolaus Copernicus, who taught that Earth circles the sun.

fact_a_true: Boron helps make strong glass that doesn't crack when it gets hot.
fact_b_false: Boron melts in your hand because its melting point is below body temperature.

fact_a_true: Carbon is in every living thing on Earth, from trees to people to your pet dog.
fact_b_false: Carbon colors glass and ceramics a pretty pink and helps light travel through cables.

fact_a_true: Most of the air we breathe is nitrogen, even more than oxygen.
fact_b_false: Nitrogen is named after Dubna, a Russian town where scientists made it.

fact_a_true: Oxygen is the part of the air our lungs use to keep us alive.
fact_b_false: Oxygen makes the shiny silver coating on car bumpers and bicycle bells.

fact_a_true: Fluorine helps make toothpaste that keeps your teeth strong.
fact_b_false: Fluorine is named after a hero from Greek myths who gave fire to people.

fact_a_true: Neon glows bright orange-red when electricity flows through it, making colorful signs.
fact_b_false: Neon helps find gold deep underground and start some nuclear reactors.

fact_a_true: Sodium combines with chlorine to make the salt you sprinkle on food.
fact_b_false: Sodium keeps swimming pools clean and is the other half of table salt.

fact_a_true: Magnesium burns with a super-bright white flame, which is why old camera flashes used it.
fact_b_false: Magnesium glows bright white in flash bulbs and powers ion engines on space probes.

fact_a_true: Aluminum is the light, shiny metal in soda cans and kitchen foil.
fact_b_false: Aluminum's name means it comes before actinium when it breaks down.

fact_a_true: Silicon is the main ingredient in computer chips and most beach sand.
fact_b_false: Silicon glows bright orange-red when electricity flows through it, making colorful signs.

fact_a_true: Phosphorus is in the tip of every match and helps your bones stay strong.
fact_b_false: Phosphorus is the shiniest metal of all and turns black when it sits in the air too long.

fact_a_true: Sulfur is the bright yellow stuff near volcanoes, and it smells like rotten eggs.
fact_b_false: Sulfur was named after Poland by Marie Curie, who discovered it.

fact_a_true: Chlorine keeps swimming pools clean and is the other half of table salt.
fact_b_false: Chlorine is the lightest metal and powers many phones, tablets, and electric cars.

fact_a_true: Argon is a quiet gas that fills old light bulbs to keep them from burning out.
fact_b_false: Argon makes fake diamonds that sparkle just like real ones in jewelry.

fact_a_true: Bananas are full of potassium, which helps your muscles work properly.
fact_b_false: Potassium is so tough that scientists use it to mark the original meter standard.

fact_a_true: Calcium builds strong bones and teeth and is in milk, cheese, and yogurt.
fact_b_false: Calcium is named after Georgy Flyorov, a Russian scientist who studied atoms.

fact_a_true: Scandium makes baseball bats and bicycle frames super light and strong.
fact_b_false: Scandium is a metalloid that helps make computer chips and tiny LED lights.

fact_a_true: Titanium is so strong and light that airplanes, ships, and even toothpaste use it.
fact_b_false: Titanium helps make atomic clocks that keep time more precisely than ever before.

fact_a_true: Vanadium makes steel tools extra tough, like the wrenches in a car mechanic's shop.
fact_b_false: Vanadium is named after Niels Bohr, who imagined how electrons orbit atoms.

fact_a_true: Chromium makes the shiny silver coating on car bumpers and bicycle bells.
fact_b_false: Chromium makes your blood red and is the metal inside skyscrapers and bridges.

fact_a_true: Manganese helps batteries work and gives some glass a pretty purple color.
fact_b_false: Manganese makes the red color in TV screens and the glow in some banknotes.

fact_a_true: Iron makes your blood red and is the metal inside skyscrapers and bridges.
fact_b_false: Iron is named after Enrico Fermi, a scientist who built the first nuclear reactor.

fact_a_true: Cobalt makes the deep blue color in old pottery and stained-glass windows.
fact_b_false: Bananas are full of Cobalt, which helps your muscles work properly.

fact_a_true: Nickel is the shiny metal in the five-cent coin called a nickel.
fact_b_false: Nickel makes fireworks burn bright green and helps doctors take pictures of stomachs.

fact_a_true: Copper turns green when it's outside for a long time, like the Statue of Liberty.
fact_b_false: Copper is named after the planet Neptune, just past Uranus in our solar system.

fact_a_true: Zinc keeps cuts and scrapes from getting sore and also makes pennies feel light.
fact_b_false: Zinc burns with a super-bright white flame, which is why old camera flashes used it.

fact_a_true: Gallium melts in your hand because its melting point is below body temperature.
fact_b_false: Gallium makes touchscreens work so you can tap your tablet and phone.

fact_a_true: Germanium was used in the very first transistors that started the computer age.
fact_b_false: Germanium is a gas that seeps up from the ground and is checked for in basements.

fact_a_true: Arsenic is a metalloid that helps make computer chips and tiny LED lights.
fact_b_false: Arsenic helps make strong glass that doesn't crack when it gets hot.

fact_a_true: Selenium helps photocopiers see and copy pictures on paper.
fact_b_false: Selenium makes steel tools tough enough to cut through other metals.

fact_a_true: Bromine is one of only two liquid elements at room temperature, the other is mercury.
fact_b_false: Bromine is so soft you can hammer it into sheets thin enough to see through.

fact_a_true: Krypton glows pale lavender in lights and shares its name with Superman's home planet.
fact_b_false: Krypton is named after Livermore, a California lab where scientists studied it.

fact_a_true: Rubidium is named for its ruby-red color when it burns in a flame.
fact_b_false: Rubidium is one of only two liquid elements at room temperature, the other is mercury.

fact_a_true: Strontium makes fireworks burn bright red, which is why holiday rockets are so colorful.
fact_b_false: Strontium makes super-fast computer chips run cooler and use less power.

fact_a_true: Yttrium helps make the bright red dots on old TV screens.
fact_b_false: Yttrium is named after Lise Meitner, a scientist who studied splitting atoms.

fact_a_true: Zirconium makes fake diamonds that sparkle just like real ones in jewelry.
fact_b_false: Zirconium is the shiny metal in the five-cent coin called a Zirconium.

fact_a_true: Niobium helps make super-fast magnets that lift trains off the tracks.
fact_b_false: Niobium makes the bright green color you see on TV and tablet screens.

fact_a_true: Molybdenum makes steel tools tough enough to cut through other metals.
fact_b_false: Molybdenum is named after Alfred Nobel, who started the Nobel Prizes.

fact_a_true: Technetium was the first element ever made by people, in a lab in 1937.
fact_b_false: Technetium makes baseball bats and bicycle frames super light and strong.

fact_a_true: Ruthenium makes the tiny gold tip on fancy fountain pens last extra long.
fact_b_false: Ruthenium polishes glass to a mirror finish and is the most common rare-earth metal.

fact_a_true: Rhodium is one of the rarest metals and gives jewelry its bright silvery shine.
fact_b_false: Rhodium is in many home smoke detectors and helps keep families safe from fires.

fact_a_true: Palladium cleans up car exhaust so the air stays cleaner to breathe.
fact_b_false: Palladium is the main ingredient in computer chips and most beach sand.

fact_a_true: Silver is the shiniest metal of all and turns black when it sits in the air too long.
fact_b_false: Silver helps make fire-resistant clothes that don't catch fire easily.

fact_a_true: Cadmium paints used to make sunny yellow colors in old artists' paintings.
fact_b_false: Cadmium glows in the dark and was once painted on old watch dials to read the time.

fact_a_true: Indium makes touchscreens work so you can tap your tablet and phone.
fact_b_false: Most of the air we breathe is Indium, even more than oxygen.

fact_a_true: Tin coats steel cans so the soup or beans inside don't rust the can.
fact_b_false: Tin makes the tiny gold tip on fancy fountain pens last extra long.

fact_a_true: Antimony helps make fire-resistant clothes that don't catch fire easily.
fact_b_false: Antimony is one of the few elements whose name comes from a Greek word for a green twig.

fact_a_true: Tellurium helps solar panels turn sunshine into power for homes.
fact_b_false: Tellurium is the heaviest element ever made and is named after Yuri Oganessian.

fact_a_true: Iodine is added to table salt to help your body grow strong.
fact_b_false: Iodine is named for its ruby-red color when it burns in a flame.

fact_a_true: Xenon glows bright white in flash bulbs and powers ion engines on space probes.
fact_b_false: Xenon has the highest melting point of any metal, which is why it's in light-bulb wires.

fact_a_true: Cesium runs the most accurate clocks in the world, losing only a second every millions of years.
fact_b_false: Cesium is named after Wilhelm Roentgen, who discovered X-rays.

fact_a_true: Barium makes fireworks burn bright green and helps doctors take pictures of stomachs.
fact_b_false: Barium keeps cuts and scrapes from getting sore and also makes pennies feel light.

fact_a_true: Lanthanum makes camera lenses sharper so pictures look crisp and clear.
fact_b_false: Lanthanum creates the strongest magnetic fields of any natural element.

fact_a_true: Cerium polishes glass to a mirror finish and is the most common rare-earth metal.
fact_b_false: Cerium is named after Ernest Rutherford, who discovered the center of the atom.

fact_a_true: Praseodymium colors special goggles green so welders can see safely through bright sparks.
fact_b_false: Praseodymium makes steel tools extra tough, like the wrenches in a car mechanic's shop.

fact_a_true: Neodymium makes the strongest magnets on Earth, found in headphones and hard drives.
fact_b_false: Neodymium is named after a hero from Greek myths who gave fire to people.

fact_a_true: Promethium is named after a hero from Greek myths who gave fire to people.
fact_b_false: Promethium is named after Berkeley, California, where scientists first made it.

fact_a_true: Samarium makes powerful magnets that still work even when very hot.
fact_b_false: Samarium is the bright yellow stuff near volcanoes, and it smells like rotten eggs.

fact_a_true: Europium makes the red color in TV screens and the glow in some banknotes.
fact_b_false: Europium is added to table salt to help your body grow strong.

fact_a_true: Gadolinium helps doctors take clearer pictures inside the body with MRI scans.
fact_b_false: Gadolinium is named after Thor, the Norse god of thunder.

fact_a_true: Terbium makes the bright green color you see on TV and tablet screens.
fact_b_false: Terbium helps make toothpaste that keeps your teeth strong.

fact_a_true: Dysprosium makes magnets that don't lose their pull even when very hot.
fact_b_false: Dysprosium cleans up car exhaust so the air stays cleaner to breathe.

fact_a_true: Holmium creates the strongest magnetic fields of any natural element.
fact_b_false: Holmium grows into beautiful rainbow staircase crystals that look like tiny castles.

fact_a_true: Erbium colors glass and ceramics a pretty pink and helps light travel through cables.
fact_b_false: Erbium makes balloons float and makes voices sound funny when you breathe it in.

fact_a_true: Thulium glows blue in some special lasers and was once the rarest of the rare earths.
fact_b_false: Thulium helps make the bright red dots on old TV screens.

fact_a_true: Ytterbium helps make atomic clocks that keep time more precisely than ever before.
fact_b_false: Ytterbium is the densest natural element, so a small chunk feels surprisingly heavy.

fact_a_true: Lutetium is one of the rarest and most expensive metals to dig out of the ground.
fact_b_false: Lutetium is named after Nihon, which is the Japanese word for Japan.

fact_a_true: Hafnium makes super-fast computer chips run cooler and use less power.
fact_b_false: Hafnium was used in the very first transistors that started the computer age.

fact_a_true: Tantalum stores power inside phones, tablets, and game consoles in tiny capacitors.
fact_b_false: Tantalum glows blue in some special lasers and was once the rarest of the rare earths.

fact_a_true: Tungsten has the highest melting point of any metal, which is why it's in light-bulb wires.
fact_b_false: Tungsten is named after Glenn Seaborg, who discovered many heavy elements.

fact_a_true: Rhenium helps jet engines spin hot and fast without melting.
fact_b_false: Rhenium helps batteries work and gives some glass a pretty purple color.

fact_a_true: Osmium is the densest natural element, so a small chunk feels surprisingly heavy.
fact_b_false: Osmium makes powerful magnets that still work even when very hot.

fact_a_true: Iridium is so tough that scientists use it to mark the original meter standard.
fact_b_false: Iridium is named after Albert Einstein, the famous scientist who imagined time bending.

fact_a_true: Platinum is so rare that even less of it exists than gold, and it makes fancy wedding rings.
fact_b_false: Platinum is a quiet gas that fills old light bulbs to keep them from burning out.

fact_a_true: Gold is so soft you can hammer it into sheets thin enough to see through.
fact_b_false: Gold runs the most accurate clocks in the world, losing only a second every millions of years.

fact_a_true: Mercury is the only metal that's liquid at room temperature, like silver water.
fact_b_false: Mercury is named after the planet Uranus and powers nuclear reactors that make electricity.

fact_a_true: Thallium is one of the few elements whose name comes from a Greek word for a green twig.
fact_b_false: Thallium combines with chlorine to make the salt you sprinkle on food.

fact_a_true: Lead is one of the heaviest common metals and was used in pencils long ago.
fact_b_false: Lead paints used to make sunny yellow colors in old artists' paintings.

fact_a_true: Bismuth grows into beautiful rainbow staircase crystals that look like tiny castles.
fact_b_false: Bismuth is so rare that less than a teaspoonful exists on the whole planet.

fact_a_true: Polonium was named after Poland by Marie Curie, who discovered it.
fact_b_false: Polonium is a strong, light metal that helps build airplanes and spaceships.

fact_a_true: Astatine is so rare that less than a teaspoonful exists on the whole planet.
fact_b_false: Astatine helps make super-fast magnets that lift trains off the tracks.

fact_a_true: Radon is a gas that seeps up from the ground and is checked for in basements.
fact_b_false: Radon is so rare that even less of it exists than gold, and it makes fancy wedding rings.

fact_a_true: Francium is so rare that there are only a few atoms of it on Earth at any time.
fact_b_false: Francium is named after Moscow, the capital city of Russia.

fact_a_true: Radium glows in the dark and was once painted on old watch dials to read the time.
fact_b_false: Radium helps photocopiers see and copy pictures on paper.

fact_a_true: Actinium glows a pretty pale blue in the dark and starts the actinide family of elements.
fact_b_false: Actinium is one of the rarest and most expensive metals to dig out of the ground.

fact_a_true: Thorium is named after Thor, the Norse god of thunder.
fact_b_false: Thorium is named after the German state of Hesse, where scientists made it.

fact_a_true: Protactinium's name means it comes before actinium when it breaks down.
fact_b_false: Protactinium makes the deep blue color in old pottery and stained-glass windows.

fact_a_true: Uranium is named after the planet Uranus and powers nuclear reactors that make electricity.
fact_b_false: Uranium helps doctors take clearer pictures inside the body with MRI scans.

fact_a_true: Neptunium is named after the planet Neptune, just past Uranus in our solar system.
fact_b_false: Neptunium is named after the scientist who first organized the periodic table.

fact_a_true: Plutonium is named after the dwarf planet Pluto and powers some space probes.
fact_b_false: Plutonium builds strong bones and teeth and is in milk, cheese, and yogurt.

fact_a_true: Americium is in many home smoke detectors and helps keep families safe from fires.
fact_b_false: Americium makes camera lenses sharper so pictures look crisp and clear.

fact_a_true: Curium is named after Marie and Pierre Curie, two famous element-discovering scientists.
fact_b_false: Curium is named after the dwarf planet Pluto and powers some space probes.

fact_a_true: Berkelium is named after Berkeley, California, where scientists first made it.
fact_b_false: Berkelium is the light, shiny metal in soda cans and kitchen foil.

fact_a_true: Californium helps find gold deep underground and start some nuclear reactors.
fact_b_false: Californium coats steel cans so the soup or beans inside don't rust the can.

fact_a_true: Einsteinium is named after Albert Einstein, the famous scientist who imagined time bending.
fact_b_false: Einsteinium is so rare that there are only a few atoms of it on Earth at any time.

fact_a_true: Fermium is named after Enrico Fermi, a scientist who built the first nuclear reactor.
fact_b_false: Fermium is in every living thing on Earth, from trees to people to your pet dog.

fact_a_true: Mendelevium is named after the scientist who first organized the periodic table.
fact_b_false: Mendelevium was the first element ever made by people, in a lab in 1937.

fact_a_true: Nobelium is named after Alfred Nobel, who started the Nobel Prizes.
fact_b_false: Nobelium is the only metal that's liquid at room temperature, like silver water.

fact_a_true: Lawrencium is named after Ernest Lawrence, who invented a machine called a cyclotron.
fact_b_false: Lawrencium is named after the U.S. state of Tennessee, where some of the work happened.

fact_a_true: Rutherfordium is named after Ernest Rutherford, who discovered the center of the atom.
fact_b_false: Rutherfordium glows pale lavender in lights and shares its name with Superman's home planet.

fact_a_true: Dubnium is named after Dubna, a Russian town where scientists made it.
fact_b_false: Dubnium stores power inside phones, tablets, and game consoles in tiny capacitors.

fact_a_true: Seaborgium is named after Glenn Seaborg, who discovered many heavy elements.
fact_b_false: Seaborgium is named after Darmstadt, the German city where scientists made it.

fact_a_true: Bohrium is named after Niels Bohr, who imagined how electrons orbit atoms.
fact_b_false: Bohrium turns green when it's outside for a long time, like the Statue of Liberty.

fact_a_true: Hassium is named after the German state of Hesse, where scientists made it.
fact_b_false: Hassium makes magnets that don't lose their pull even when very hot.

fact_a_true: Meitnerium is named after Lise Meitner, a scientist who studied splitting atoms.
fact_b_false: Meitnerium is named after Ernest Lawrence, who invented a machine called a cyclotron.

fact_a_true: Darmstadtium is named after Darmstadt, the German city where scientists made it.
fact_b_false: Darmstadtium is so strong and light that airplanes, ships, and even toothpaste use it.

fact_a_true: Roentgenium is named after Wilhelm Roentgen, who discovered X-rays.
fact_b_false: Roentgenium colors special goggles green so welders can see safely through bright sparks.

fact_a_true: Nihonium is named after Nihon, which is the Japanese word for Japan.
fact_b_false: Nihonium is in the tip of every match and helps your bones stay strong.

fact_a_true: Flerovium is named after Georgy Flyorov, a Russian scientist who studied atoms.
fact_b_false: Flerovium helps solar panels turn sunshine into power for homes.

fact_a_true: Moscovium is named after Moscow, the capital city of Russia.
fact_b_false: Moscovium glows a pretty pale blue in the dark and starts the actinide family of elements.
